question: How do I properly set up an LLC in Texas?
answer:
Step 1: Choose a unique business name (including an LLC designator) and check availability.
Step 2: File a Certificate of Formation – Limited Liability Company (Form 205) with the Texas Secretary of State (SOS).
Step 3: Designate a registered agent and registered office in Texas.
Step 4: Draft a Company/Operating Agreement to govern internal affairs (strongly recommended).
Step 5: Maintain statutory records and meet ongoing obligations such as Texas franchise tax filings.
sources: https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/forms/205_boc.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com|https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/businessstructure.shtml?utm_source=chatgpt.com

question: How do I check if my LLC name is available in Texas?
answer:
Step 1: Brainstorm a few name options that include an LLC designator (like “LLC” or “L.L.C.”).
Step 2: Check Texas Secretary of State name availability resources (and consider reserving a name if needed).
Step 3: Avoid names that are deceptively similar or require special approvals.
Step 4: If you’ll use a different public-facing brand name, plan for an assumed name (DBA) filing.

question: How much does it cost to file a Texas LLC (Form 205) and where do I file it?
answer:
Step 1: Prepare the Certificate of Formation – LLC (Form 205).
Step 2: File with the Texas Secretary of State (often via SOSDirect, or by other accepted methods).
Step 3: Pay the state filing fee (Form 205 lists a $300 filing fee).
Step 4: Keep your stamped/approved copies in your company records.
sources: https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/forms/205_boc.pdf|https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/forms_boc.shtml

question: Does my Texas LLC need an EIN and how do I get one?
answer:
Step 1: Many LLCs need an EIN (especially if you have employees, multiple members, or want to open certain bank accounts).
Step 2: Apply directly on the IRS website (it’s free).
Step 3: Save the EIN confirmation for banking and tax registrations.
Step 4: Watch out for third-party sites that charge fees for an EIN.
sources: https://www.irs.gov/businesses/small-businesses-self-employed/get-an-employer-identification-number|https://www.irs.gov/forms-pubs/about-form-ss-4
followups: Will you have employees?|Is your LLC single-member or multi-member?

question: What is the Texas franchise tax and when is it due?
answer:
Step 1: Assume your LLC will have Texas franchise tax obligations unless an exemption applies.
Step 2: Plan for an annual filing cycle.
Step 3: The Texas Comptroller’s guidance says the annual franchise tax report is due May 15 (or the next business day if it falls on a weekend/holiday).
Step 4: Keep your entity info and ownership details current to file accurately.
sources: https://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/franchise/|https://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/98-806.php
followups: Do you expect revenue under the no-tax-due threshold?|What is your accounting year end?

question: Do I need a Texas sales tax permit and how do I apply?
answer:
Step 1: If you sell, lease, or rent taxable goods/services (or owe use tax), you likely need a Texas sales tax permit.
Step 2: Apply through the Texas Comptroller (online registration system or paper application).
Step 3: After approval, collect and remit sales tax as required.
Step 4: Keep up with reporting and payment requirements for your permit.
sources: https://comptroller.texas.gov/help/sales-tax-registration/requirements.php/|https://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/permit/|https://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/sales/faq/permit.php
followups: What are you selling (goods/services)?|Will you sell online, in-person, or both?

question: Do I need a DBA (assumed name) in Texas for my business?
answer:
Step 1: If you will operate under a name that’s different from your LLC’s legal name, you may need an assumed name (DBA) filing.
Step 2: Texas SOS provides assumed name filing information and Form 503.
Step 3: Depending on your setup, you may also need to file at the county level where you conduct business.
Step 4: Track renewal/validity periods and keep copies in your records.
sources: https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/namefilingsfaqs.shtml|https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/forms/503_boc.pdf
followups: Is your brand name different from your legal LLC name?|Which county/city will you operate in?

question: If I hire employees, what do I need to do for Texas unemployment tax?
answer:
Step 1: Determine when you become a liable employer under Texas rules.
Step 2: Register for a Texas Workforce Commission (TWC) unemployment tax account.
Step 3: TWC guidance says employers must register within 10 days of becoming liable.
Step 4: File wage reports and pay unemployment tax as required.
sources: https://www.twc.texas.gov/services/register-tax|https://www.twc.texas.gov/programs/unemployment-tax
followups: How many employees do you expect in the first 90 days?|Are you paying W-2 wages or using contractors?

question: Do I have to report new hires in Texas and what is the deadline?
answer:
Step 1: If you hire employees, you’ll generally need to report new hires and rehires.
Step 2: Texas guidance states employers must report new hires/rehires within 20 calendar days of the hire date.
Step 3: Set a process: once a worker completes a W-4, file the new hire report.
Step 4: Keep proof of submission for your records.
sources: https://www.twc.texas.gov/employer-resources/new-hire-reporting|https://www.texasattorneygeneral.gov/child-support/employers/new-hire-reporting
followups: How many hires are you planning soon?|Are any hires remote/out-of-state?

question: Do I need to file a Beneficial Ownership Information (BOI) report for my Texas LLC?
answer:
Step 1: BOI requirements have changed recently and depend on whether the entity is a U.S. company or a foreign company registered to do business in the U.S.
Step 2: FinCEN’s BOI page notes an interim final rule that removes the BOI reporting requirement for U.S. companies and U.S. persons (while foreign companies may still have obligations).
Step 3: Verify your status directly on FinCEN before you assume you’re exempt.
Step 4: If you’re unsure, confirm with counsel because this area is evolving.
sources: https://www.fincen.gov/boi|https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/cta.shtml
followups: Is your entity formed in the U.S. or outside the U.S.?|Are you registering a foreign entity in Texas?

question: What is SOSDirect and when should I use it to form a Texas business?
answer:
Step 1: Use SOSDirect to search and file Texas business documents online with the Secretary of State.
Step 2: It’s commonly used for formation filings, assumed name filings, and ordering records/certificates.
Step 3: Expect small per-search fees; save receipts and confirmations.
Step 4: For faster processing, review SOS filing options and expedited services.
sources: https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/sosda/index.shtml|https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/options.shtml
followups: Are you filing online or by mail?|Do you need expedited processing?

question: How long is an assumed name (DBA) valid in Texas when filed with the Secretary of State?
answer:
Step 1: Form 503 allows an assumed name period up to 10 years.
Step 2: Pick a duration and track the expiration date.
Step 3: Keep filed copies with your records.
Step 4: Renew/replace before expiration if you still use the name.
sources: https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/forms/503_boc.pdf|https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/instructions/503.shtml

question: Do I need to choose member-managed or manager-managed for a Texas LLC?
answer:
Step 1: Texas LLCs can be managed by members or by managers.
Step 2: The management structure must be stated in the certificate of formation.
Step 3: Member-managed fits owner-operated businesses; manager-managed fits a delegated management setup.
Step 4: Mirror this in your operating agreement and internal records.
sources: https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/businessstructure.shtml|https://www.sos.state.tx.us/corp/forms/205_boc.pdf
followups: Will owners run day-to-day operations?|Will there be passive members?

question: If I hire employees, do I have to complete Form I-9 and what’s the deadline?
answer:
Step 1: Employers must complete and retain Form I-9 for each person hired for employment in the U.S.
Step 2: Employees complete Section 1 no later than the first day of employment.
Step 3: Employers complete Section 2 within 3 business days of the employee’s first day of work.
Step 4: Follow USCIS guidance for verification and retention.
sources: https://www.uscis.gov/i-9-central/completing-form-i-9|https://www.uscis.gov/i-9-central/completing-form-i-9/completing-section-2-employer-review-and-attestation

Step 2: Reform Requirements Small businesses with fewer than 50 full-time plus full-time equivalent employees won’t face a penalty if they don’t provide health insurance to their employees. Federal law defines a full-time employee as one who works at least 30 hours during a typical work week. The law counts each 120 hours worked by part-time employees in a month as one full-time equivalent employee.
Step 3: Insurance Commercial Property Insurance helps businesses, including farms and ranches, pay to repair or replace buildings and other property damaged or destroyed because of fire, storm, or other incidents covered by the owner’s policy. It also pays to replace stolen or lost property. Business owners can buy commercial property insurance regardless of whether they own, rent, or lease a building.
